figures.name: Dievas / Dievs
figures.gloss: The bright sky and high god of the Balts, whose very name is the unbroken cognate of *Dyeus — the same word as Zeus, Jupiter, Dyaus, Tiwaz, worn smooth across five thousand years.
figures.facet: The shard that kept the word: where Tyr decayed and Zeus was renamed, the Baltic peasant still said *Dievas* into the twentieth century — the longest-surviving utterance of the Sky-Father's true name.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from Lithuanian and Latvian folksong (dainas) collected 18th–20th c.; the late Christianisation of the Balts preserved an unusually archaic layer.

figures.name: Perkūnas / Pērkons
figures.gloss: The thunder-god who rides the sky in a fiery chariot and splits the oak, his name from *Perkwunos — the near-twin of Slavic Perun and structural brother to Indra and Thor.
figures.facet: Keeper of the oldest storm-myth still living in folklore: Perkūnas hunts Velnias the serpent across the sky and through every tree and stone the devil hides behind — the IE thunder-vs-serpent combat told as a peasant chase.

figures.name: Saulė / Saule
figures.gloss: The Sun as a goddess and mother, who drives her copper-wheeled chariot across the sky, weeps amber tears into the sea, and tends the cosmic apple-tree at the world's edge.
figures.facet: The divergence that overturns a habit: where most pantheons make the sun a king or charioteer-god, the Balts kept the Sun female and maternal — a feminine solar eye, complement not consort to the moon.

figures.name: Mėnulis / Mēness
figures.gloss: The Moon, husband of the Sun, who weds Saulė at the world's beginning and then, faithless, courts the dawn-star Aušrinė — for which Perkūnas cleaves him, and his wound is the moon's waning.
figures.facet: A cosmic divorce written into the calendar: the Sun-and-Moon marriage, their quarrel, and the Moon's punishment explain the phases as the scar of an infidelity — myth as the reckoning of time itself.

figures.name: Žemyna
figures.gloss: The Earth-Mother, the blossoming ground from whom all life rises and to whom it returns; her name shares the root *dhg'hem- with Greek chthon and Latin humus — the Earth herself.
figures.facet: Kissed at dawn and at death: the Lithuanian farmer greeted Žemyna by kissing the soil and poured the first of every drink to her — a great-mother worshipped not in temple but in the daily act of touching the ground.

figures.name: Laima
figures.gloss: Goddess of fate, fortune and birth, who lays down each life's measure at the threshold; her decree is *lemtis*, the allotted portion no god overturns.
figures.facet: Fate as a midwife, not a spinner: Laima stands at the moment of birth to speak the lot, and even Dievas defers to what she has set — the impersonal allotment that order itself obeys.

figures.name: Velnias / Velns
figures.gloss: Lord of the dead, of cattle, water and the dark below — the serpentine chthonic power whom Perkūnas eternally hunts; later flattened by Christianity into the folk-devil.
figures.facet: The slandered sovereign: Velnias was not evil but the underworld's rightful master of wealth and the dead, demoted to 'the Devil' only when the new faith needed an enemy for its God — a shard cracked by conversion.
figures.provenance: The pre-Christian Velnias/Vels must be read beneath the later Christianised folk-devil; the chthonic, cattle-keeping layer is the genuinely archaic one (cf. Latvian Veļi, the dead).

figures.name: Dieva dēli
figures.gloss: The 'Sons of God', the Latvian divine twins who court Saule's daughter, rescue her from the sea and steady her sinking boat — the Baltic face of the Indo-European Dawn-Twins.
figures.facet: A living witness to a reconstruction: the Dieva dēli courting the Sun-Maiden mirror the Vedic Aśvins and Greek Dioskouroi so closely that they confirm the *Diwos sunu — Sons of Dyeus — as a real inherited myth, not a guess.

figures.name: Aušrinė
figures.gloss: The Morning Star, the dawn-goddess who kindles Saulė's fire and lights her way; her name from *h2ews-, the same root as Greek Eos, Latin Aurora, Vedic Uṣas.
figures.facet: The dawn the Balts kept by name: where Eos and Aurora became literary figures, Aušrinė stayed a working morning-star whose mythic root is audible in the word itself.

edges.note: Lithuanian Dievas / Latvian Dievs continues *Dyeus directly — among the least-altered reflexes of the reconstructed Sky-Father's name.
Same Indo-European etymon *Dyeus; Dievas and Zeus are the same inherited word in different daughter tongues.
Dievas and Iuppiter (Dieus-pater) share the *Dyeus root by regular sound-law within Indo-European.
Dievas and Vedic Dyaus continue the same *Dyeus — the cognate star at its widest span.
Both from *Dyeus/*Tiwaz; Dievas kept the supreme sky-god role that Tyr surrendered to Odin and Thor.
Perkūnas and Perun both reflect *Perkwunos within the Balto-Slavic node — the closest cognate pair in the whole storm-cluster, oak and thunder shared.
The thunder-god who slays the serpent: Perkūnas hunting Velnias rhymes structurally with Indra and Vṛtra, though the names are not cognate.
Oak-and-thunder god wielding the bolt against a chthonic serpent — the same IE storm-template, distinct lineage from Perkūnas's *Perkwunos.
Wheel-and-thunder gods of the western and eastern IE world; both carry the *(s)tenh2- / *Perkwunos thunder-complex, no proven common name.
The defining Baltic combat: Perkūnas (sky) eternally pursues Velnias (the serpent below) — the IE thunder-vs-serpent myth preserved as living folklore.
Sun-wife and Moon-husband, their marriage and quarrel are the Baltic cosmogony of day, night and the lunar phases.
Two of the rare feminine solar deities — Saulė and Amaterasu — independent witnesses that the sun need not be male.
Both drive the solar chariot across the sky and both belong to IE solar imagery, but Saulė is mother where Sūrya is lord — the gender inverts.
Chariot-driving sun of the IE world; the Baltic Saulė keeps the wheel and the daily course while turning the charioteer female.
The Moon's faithless courting of the dawn-star Aušrinė is the offence for which Perkūnas wounds him — the myth that reads the lunar phases.
Within the Baltic sky-family Aušrinė the morning-star serves and kindles Saulė the Sun, attested together in the dainas.
Earth personified as mother and source; Žemyna and Gaia share the function and, with chthon, the *dhg'hem- earth-root — but the divine figures are parallels, not one name.
Grain-and-ground mothers of the field; the Lithuanian first-fruits to Žemyna echo Demeter's agrarian cult without proven descent.
The Dieva dēli court and rescue Saule's daughter and right her sinking boat — the Baltic Sons-of-God / Sun-Maiden cycle in one scene.
The 'Sons of God' are by name the sons of Dievas — *Diwos sunu, the divine twins begotten of the Sky-Father.
Aušrinė's root *h2ews- (dawn) is cognate with Greek Eos/Aurora, not with Helios; the seam belongs to the dawn-goddess line — flagged as a reach, true target is the dawn not the sun.
Goddesses presiding over birth, marriage and the measure of a life; Laima at the threshold rhymes with Frigg who knows all fates yet speaks none.
Both touch the allotment of life and death, but Laima is a benevolent midwife of fate where the Morrigan is its war-shadow — a loose, divergent parallel.
Wealth-keeping lords of the dead below; Velnias, master of cattle and the buried hoard, parallels Hades the rich one — independent underworld sovereigns.
First-and-foremost lords of the dead in IE daughter traditions; the Latvian Veļi (the dead) gather under Velnias as the departed gather under Yama.
